reasoning: The code pattern is exactly as described: CONTENT_TYPE_BOUNDARY_RE is declared at module scope with the /gi flags on line 43, and in parse() (lines 187-191) lastIndex is manually set to m.index + m[0].length - 1 before calling exec(). However, the exploit scenario does not hold in standard Node.js. The lastIndex assignment and exec() call are entirely synchronous with no async gap or event loop tick between them, so in single-threaded Node.js they cannot be interleaved by another request. The claimed risk scenario (Worker Threads sharing the same module instance) is a non-default, unusual deployment pattern that most users do not employ. The input (contentType from req.headers) is attacker-controlled and there is no schema validation guard, but this does not matter because the synchronous nature prevents any actual corruption in normal use. The finding is a genuine code quality observation about a fragile pattern, but it does not constitute a reachable exploitable vulnerability in standard deployments. The description itself concedes it is 'effectively a code quality/fragility issue in standard single-threaded Node.js.' Per the adversarial verification standard ('Default to is_real=false unless the code proves it'), the burden is not met for a real exploitable finding.
